content:
9 min read
2 hours ago

--

The biggest improvements came from reducing unnecessary work in the dependency graph, application boundaries, and CI pipeline, not from replacing Next.js.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

We blamed Next.js because the build command was where the waiting happened.
The actual causes were spread across the entire project.
Our build had become slow enough to change how the team worked. Pull requests waited longer for CI. Failed deployments became more expensive because feedback arrived late. Developers delayed small releases, grouped unrelated changes together, and became less willing to experiment near deployment time.
The framework became the obvious suspect because the delay appeared after running next build.
But the build was only executing the system we had created.
The problem was not one catastrophically slow compiler step. It was the accumulation of broad dependencies, generated output, duplicated validation, ineffective caches, and scripts that had quietly become mandatory for every deployment.
No single decision looked disastrous.
Together, they turned build time into a daily engineering tax.
The useful question was not:
Is Next.js too slow for this application?
It was:
What work does every build perform, and which parts are actually necessary?
That question changed the investigation.
We stopped treating the build as one command and started treating it as a dependency system.
1. The Build Became a Workflow Problem, Not Just a Speed Problem
A slow build does not only waste the number of minutes displayed in CI.
It changes developer behavior.
When feedback is fast, developers make smaller changes, deploy more often, and correct mistakes while the context is still fresh. When feedback is slow, they begin optimizing their behavior around the delay.
A five-minute increase can produce much more than five minutes of cost.
Developers start batching changes because each deployment feels expensive. Pull requests become larger because waiting for another CI cycle is frustrating. Riskier changes remain open longer. Failed builds interrupt work that has already moved on to something else.
Eventually, the build stops being infrastructure and becomes part of the team’s decision-making process.
That was the first sign that we needed to investigate the system, not merely complain about the command.
Before changing anything, we defined what “slow” actually meant.
We measured several different paths:

• A clean local production build

• A local build with existing caches

• The full CI workflow

• Dependency installation

• Deployment packaging and upload

• Time from commit to deployable artifact
These measurements were not interchangeable.
A developer might see an acceptable local build because dependencies were already installed and caches were warm. CI might still be slow because it recreated the environment, downloaded tools, regenerated files, and repeated checks in separate jobs.
Similarly, reducing next build by one minute would not matter much if dependency installation and duplicated validation consumed most of the pipeline.
Without a baseline, build optimization becomes a collection of beliefs.
With a baseline, it becomes a list of costs.
2. We Measured the Build Before Trying to Fix It
The original complaint was simple:
The Next.js build is slow.
That statement was emotionally accurate but technically incomplete.
A deployment pipeline usually contains much more than the framework build:

• Restoring caches

• Installing dependencies

• Generating clients, schemas, or content

• Running linting

• Running type checks

• Compiling application code

• Generating static pages

• Processing assets

• Packaging output

• Uploading deployment artifacts
Some of these stages were visible. Others were hidden inside scripts with harmless names such as prepare, validate, or build:production.
We added timing around each major step and inspected CI jobs separately. That exposed an important pattern: the longest visible command did not own all of the avoidable cost.
Some validation was running before the framework build and again during another pipeline stage. Generated files were recreated even when their inputs had not changed. Multiple jobs installed the same dependency graph independently. A package build ran because the workspace had changed, even when that package’s inputs had not.
The pipeline was not slow because one operation had become impossibly expensive.
It was slow because many reasonable operations had become unconditional.
This distinction matters.
A slow compiler may require a compiler-level solution. Unnecessary work requires an architectural or pipeline-level solution.
Before optimization, “the build is slow” was an opinion.
After measurement, it became a collection of specific costs.
3. Our Dependency Graph Was Larger Than the Application Change
One of the first architectural problems appeared in imports that looked completely normal during code review.
The project had accumulated broad shared entry points:
import { formatCurrency, validateUser, trackEvent } from "@/shared";That import was convenient. It was also hiding how much of the project sat behind @/shared.
The entry point exported browser utilities, server helpers, validation schemas, analytics integrations, configuration, formatting functions, and shared components. A route might need one formatter, but its dependency path passed through a module connected to many unrelated parts of the application.
A narrower import made the real relationship visible:
import { formatCurrency } from "@/shared/formatters/currency";This is not an argument that barrel files are always bad or that every import must point to an internal file.
The issue was dependency expansion.
The build did not understand that our code change was conceptually small. It only understood that the changed module was connected to a large portion of the application.
That connection affected more than output size. It influenced module traversal, cache invalidation, package rebuilding, and the amount of code the tooling needed to analyze.
The same pattern appeared in workspace packages.
Applications imported from package roots because those roots were convenient. The package roots exported nearly everything. A change to one internal area could affect consumers that did not actually depend on that area conceptually.
We started treating public package entry points as architectural boundaries rather than export conveniences.
Packages exposed deliberate surfaces. Frequently changing product logic was separated from stable primitives. Server-only modules were no longer re-exported through universal entry points. Features stopped importing unrelated feature internals through shared shortcuts.
The result was not merely a faster build.
The codebase became easier to explain.
Build performance improved because the dependency graph began matching the way developers already believed the application was structured.
4. Shared Code Was Making Unrelated Routes Pay Together
Most large applications eventually create folders named shared, common, utils, or lib.
These folders begin as attempts to reduce duplication.
Over time, they often become dependency hubs.
A helper is placed in shared because two features need it. Another helper arrives because the location already exists. Soon, the shared layer contains authentication utilities, API clients, UI primitives, browser storage, server configuration, analytics, formatting, and business rules.
The folder is reusable, but it no longer has a coherent responsibility.
This affected our build because unrelated routes were connected through frequently changing shared modules. A small update to product logic could invalidate work across routes that did not use the changed behavior directly.
Tree shaking did not make this architectural problem disappear.
Tree shaking can remove unused code from runtime output. It does not automatically make broad coupling easy to understand, cache, invalidate, or maintain. The build system still has to analyze modules and determine what is used.
We did not solve this by eliminating shared code.
We divided it by stability, ownership, and execution environment.
Stable formatting primitives were separated from product rules. Server-only code had explicit boundaries. Browser utilities did not flow through modules also used by server rendering. Feature-specific helpers remained inside their features until a genuinely stable shared abstraction emerged.
This reduced the number of routes that depended on frequently changing modules.
It also improved ownership.
When everything is shared, every change appears globally important. Developers become cautious because the blast radius is unclear. Tests become broader because boundaries are weak. Code review becomes slower because nobody knows whether an innocent change affects one screen or twenty.
The architecture had been optimized for import convenience.
We changed it to optimize for understandable relationships.
The same boundaries that made the code easier to maintain also made the build easier to reason about.
5. The Pipeline Repeated Work That Had Already Been Done
After improving application boundaries, we examined the CI pipeline.
This exposed a different category of waste: work that was valid but repeated.
Dependencies were installed in multiple jobs. Full type checks ran in more than one stage. Code generation recreated identical artifacts. Workspace packages rebuilt because the repository changed, not because their inputs changed.
Some checks ran serially even though they were independent.
The first instinct was to add more caching.
That would have been another incomplete solution.
Caching only helps when the cached unit represents reusable work.
A useful cache requires:

• Deterministic output

• Stable but correct cache keys

• Clear inputs

• Correct invalidation

• Restoration that is cheaper than recomputation

• Boundaries that match how the project changes
Our earlier caches technically existed, but their keys changed too often. A small edit invalidated large outputs. Other caches were so broad that reusing them safely was difficult.
We stopped asking where caching could be added.
We started asking which work produced reusable output.
That changed the design.
Generated artifacts were skipped when their actual inputs were unchanged. Package caches were keyed to package-level dependencies rather than the entire repository. Tools already available in the build image were no longer downloaded repeatedly. Independent validation ran in parallel where failure behavior remained clear.
We also removed duplicated checks.
Running linting, type checking, tests, and the production build can be valuable. Running the same validation through several overlapping scripts usually is not.
The framework build did not need to become responsible for proving everything. The pipeline needed clear ownership for each form of validation.
A cache miss should mean something relevant changed.
Previously, it often meant our cache boundaries were too vague to describe what could be reused.
6. Several Small Fixes Beat One Dramatic Rewrite
There was no single optimization that transformed the build.
The final improvement looked dramatic because several ordinary bottlenecks were removed one at a time.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Each change followed the same process.
First, identify a suspected cost.
Then measure whether it was real.
Change one boundary or stage.
Measure again.
Keep the change only if the reduction justified the added complexity.
This last step mattered because build optimization can create its own maintenance burden. A complicated cache strategy may save time today while becoming impossible to debug later. Aggressive parallelization can make failures harder to interpret. Deep import paths can expose internals that packages should keep private.
The goal was not the shortest possible build at any co
